patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: use lockless read in nr_cached_objects shrinker callback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch addresses a performance issue in the btrfs filesystem by replacing a lock-holding read operation with a lockless one, improving scalability under heavy memory reclaim conditions.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Gave a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 02:23:46PM -0700, Boris Burkov wrote:
> From: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>
> 
> Under heavy memcg-driven slab reclaim with many memcgs and CPUs,
> shrink_slab_memcg() invokes the per-superblock count callback once
> per (memcg, NUMA node) tuple. For btrfs that callback reaches
> percpu_counter_sum_positive() on fs_info->evictable_extent_maps,
> which takes the percpu_counter's raw spinlock with IRQs disabled and
> walks every online CPU. With hundreds of memcgs driving reclaim on a
> host with dozens of CPUs, this counter lock becomes a global
> serialization point: profiles show CPU pinned in the spin_lock_irqsave
> acquire under __percpu_counter_sum, with cross-CPU IPIs hitting
> csd_lock_wait_toolong while waiting for spinning vCPUs.
> 
> The shrinker count is advisory -- super_cache_count() already notes
> "counts can change between super_cache_count and super_cache_scan, so
> we really don't need locks here." Use percpu_counter_read_positive(),
> which is lockless. Worst-case skew is bounded by batch * num_online_cpus
> (a few thousand), negligible compared to the millions of extent maps a
> busy filesystem accumulates and well within the noise that the shrinker
> already tolerates.
> 
> Tested-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>

Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Gave a Reviewed-by tag without any specific comments or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/5/30 06:53, Boris Burkov \u5199\u9053:
> From: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>
> 
> Under heavy memcg-driven slab reclaim with many memcgs and CPUs,
> shrink_slab_memcg() invokes the per-superblock count callback once
> per (memcg, NUMA node) tuple. For btrfs that callback reaches
> percpu_counter_sum_positive() on fs_info->evictable_extent_maps,
> which takes the percpu_counter's raw spinlock with IRQs disabled and
> walks every online CPU. With hundreds of memcgs driving reclaim on a
> host with dozens of CPUs, this counter lock becomes a global
> serialization point: profiles show CPU pinned in the spin_lock_irqsave
> acquire under __percpu_counter_sum, with cross-CPU IPIs hitting
> csd_lock_wait_toolong while waiting for spinning vCPUs.
> 
> The shrinker count is advisory -- super_cache_count() already notes
> "counts can change between super_cache_count and super_cache_scan, so
> we really don't need locks here." Use percpu_counter_read_positive(),
> which is lockless. Worst-case skew is bounded by batch * num_online_cpus
> (a few thousand), negligible compared to the millions of extent maps a
> busy filesystem accumulates and well within the noise that the shrinker
> already tolerates.
> 
> Tested-by: Boris Burkov <boris@bur.io>
> Signed-off-by: Ben Maurer <bmaurer@meta.com>

Reviewed-by: Qu Wenruo <wqu@suse.com>

Thanks,
Qu

> ---
>   fs/btrfs/super.c | 2 +-
>   1 file changed, 1 insertion(+), 1 deletion(-)
> 
> diff --git a/fs/btrfs/super.c b/fs/btrfs/super.c
> index 958dd185c0d6..c946bccf0748 100644
> --- a/fs/btrfs/super.c
> +++ b/fs/btrfs/super.c
> @@ -2432,7 +2432,7 @@ static int btrfs_show_devname(struct seq_file *m, struct dentry *root)
>   static long btrfs_nr_cached_objects(struct super_block *sb, struct shrink_control *sc)
>   {
>   	struct btrfs_fs_info *fs_info = btrfs_sb(sb);
> -	const s64 nr = percpu_counter_sum_positive(&fs_info->evictable_extent_maps);
> +	const s64 nr = percpu_counter_read_positive(&fs_info->evictable_extent_maps);
>   
>   	trace_btrfs_extent_map_shrinker_count(fs_info, nr);
>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

version_history.subject: [PATCH 00/10] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs
[PATCH v2 0/9] nfsd: a pile of fixes for random bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] nfsd: fix BUG_ON in nfsd4_alloc_layout_stateid on racing delegation revoke
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server that can cause a BUG_ON when trying to allocate a layout stateid for a delegated file. The issue occurs when a concurrent delegation revoke is triggered, causing the fi_deleg_file pointer to be cleared under the fi_lock lock. To fix this, the patch takes an RCU read lock around the fi_deleg_file read in the SC_TYPE_DELEG path and replaces the BUG_ON with a graceful error return that cleans up any partially initialized layout stateid. This ensures that the NFS server can handle concurrent delegation revokes without crashing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] nfsd: RCU-protect cl_cb_session to fix use-after-free on session teardown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFS daemon by converting the cl_cb_session pointer to an RCU-protected pointer. The bug occurs when a session is destroyed while rpciod still holds an inflight callback task that dereferences the freed session. The patch uses RCU (Read-Copy Update) to ensure that readers on rpciod do not access freed memory. It also moves assignments of cl_cb_session to after successful creation of a backchannel and clears it on error returns, and switches from kfree() to kfree_rcu() for freeing the session slab.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] nfsd: convert nfsd_net boolean flags to unsigned long flags word
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the boolean flags in nfsd_net to a single unsigned long flags word, using an enum to define the bit positions. This change fixes a race condition in the grace_ended flag by using test_and_set_bit(), which is atomic on all architectures. The remaining flags are converted to use test_bit/set_bit/clear_bit for consistency and to avoid sub-word cmpxchg issues on certain architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] nfsd: dedup nfs4_client_to_reclaim inserts
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd kernel module where the nfs4_client_to_reclaim function unconditionally allocates new memory for each client, leading to incorrect behavior and potential crashes when the system is rebooted. The fix involves looking up existing clients using nfsd4_find_reclaim_client before allocating new memory, and adding a read-write semaphore to serialize access to the reclaim table. This ensures that the correct records are updated and avoids unnecessary memory allocations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] nfsd: gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue in the NFSv3 ACL implementation where set_posix_acl() was being called unconditionally, leading to incorrect behavior when setting or removing ACLs. The fix involves adding checks for the argp->mask bits before calling set_posix_acl(), ensuring that only the relevant ACLs are modified. This change mirrors the existing logic in nfsd3_proc_getacl().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] NFSD: check truncate permission under inode lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a timing issue in the NFS server where a client can extend a file before the server checks for truncation permission. The patch splits the write-count acquisition from the truncation permission check, ensuring that the correct size is used when checking for permission. This fix closes a TOCTOU window and prevents concurrent appends from extending the file before the server takes inode lock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] nfsd: fix partial-write detection in nfsd_direct_write
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd_direct_write function where it incorrectly detects short writes and continues writing to the wrong file offset. The issue arises from the fact that vfs_iocb_iter_write advances the iter by the number of bytes written, making segments[i].iter.count hold the residual length instead of the original request length. To fix this, a snapshot of the segment's byte count is taken before the write and compared against host_err to accurately detect short writes. This ensures that any short write breaks the loop and prevents over-reporting *cnt to the NFS client.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] nfsd: cap decoded POSIX ACL count to bound sort cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a performance issue in the NFS server by capping the number of POSIX ACL entries that can be decoded from a client request. The decoder previously allowed an unlimited number of entries, which led to unbounded CPU usage due to an O(n^2) bubble sort operation. The patch introduces a check to reject requests with more than the maximum allowed number of entries (NFS_ACL_MAX_ENTRIES), preventing excessive CPU usage and ensuring that ACLs set via NFSv4 POSIX ACL extensions are retrievable via NFSACL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] nfsd: validate symlink target length in NFSv4 CREATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a security vulnerability in the NFSv4 protocol by validating the length of symbolic link targets to prevent a client from forcing an unbounded allocation of memory.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses various bugs and issues in the NFS daemon, including remotely-triggerable panics, use-after-free errors, and denial-of-service vulnerabilities. The patches also include protocol fixes and improvements to the NFSv4 implementation. Specifically, the series includes changes to fix a double-put bug in fuse_try_move_page(), validate symlink target lengths, cap decoded POSIX ACL counts, and gate nfs3 setacl by argp->mask.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: add netlink support for fetching server statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for fetching NFS server statistics using Generic Netlink, a new feature in the kernel. When netlink is available, nfsstat uses it to retrieve statistics from the server. If netlink is not available (e.g., on older kernels), it falls back to parsing /proc/net/rpc/nfsd as before. The patch adds code to send a dump command and parse the reply to populate various stat arrays, including reply cache, filehandle, IO, network, RPC, and per-version procedure call counts.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Patch introduces a check for svc pool percpu counter allocation, but reviewer Jeff Layton suggests avoiding the hassle of struct randomization and keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: Expressed concerns that struct randomization could break the patch and suggested keeping it as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls to avoid hassle.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Agreed with Jeff that the patch is 'too clever by half', implying disapproval of its approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/30/26 5:48 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 17:45 -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
>> On Sat, 2026-05-30 at 16:21 -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
>>> From: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>>
>>> __svc_create() initializes three per-pool percpu_counter stats and
>>> ignores every return value. On SMP, percpu_counter_init() fails when
>>> __alloc_percpu_gfp() cannot satisfy the allocation, leaving the failed
>>> counter with fbc->counters == NULL and its embedded raw_spinlock_t,
>>> list_head, and count never initialized. __svc_create() returns the
>>> half-constructed svc_serv to nfsd, lockd, or the NFS callback service
>>> anyway.
>>>
>>> Once that service is live, the hot-path increments in
>>> svc_xprt_enqueue(), svc_handle_xprt(), and
>>> svc_pool_wake_idle_thread() reach a counter whose backing pointer is
>>> NULL. The pointer is a per-cpu offset, so the access does not fault:
>>> it resolves to offset zero of the current CPU's per-cpu area and
>>> silently corrupts whatever variable lives there. A
>>> /proc/fs/nfsd/pool_stats read walks the same NULL per-cpu storage and
>>> returns garbage, and on CONFIG_DEBUG_SPINLOCK or lockdep it splats on
>>> the never-initialized lock.
>>>
>>> Creating the broken service requires a percpu allocation failure during
>>> RPC server startup, so it is reachable only by a local administrator
>>> under memory pressure or fault injection; a remote peer cannot induce
>>> the bad state on its own.
>>>
>>> Initialize the three adjacent pool counters with one checked
>>> percpu_counter_init_many() call and fail __svc_create() when the
>>> allocation fails, unwinding the counters for pools already set up. Use
>>> the matching percpu_counter_destroy_many() at teardown so the single
>>> per-cpu allocation is freed exactly once.
>>>
>>> Fixes: ccf08bed6e7a ("SUNRPC: Replace pool stats with per-CPU variables")
>>> Signed-off-by: Chuck Lever <chuck.lever@oracle.com>
>>> ---
>>>  net/sunrpc/svc.c | 32 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++------
>>>  1 file changed, 26 insertions(+), 6 deletions(-)
>>>
>>> diff --git a/net/sunrpc/svc.c b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> index ae9ec4bf34f7..aeb6e631848c 100644
>>> --- a/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> +++ b/net/sunrpc/svc.c
>>> @@ -476,6 +476,22 @@ __svc_init_bc(struct svc_serv *serv)
>>>  }
>>>  #endif
>>>  
>>> +enum {
>>> +	SVC_POOL_COUNTERS = 3,
>>> +};
>>> +
>>> +static int svc_pool_init_counters(struct svc_pool *pool)
>>> +{
>>> +	return percpu_counter_init_many(&pool->sp_messages_arrived, 0,
>>> +					GFP_KERNEL, SVC_POOL_COUNTERS);
>>> +}
>>>
>>>
>>
>> Switching to this looks like a good thing, but it means that the
>> svc_pool struct fields now have some strict ordering requirements. The
>> percpu_counters all need to be snuggled up together.
>>
>> That deserves a comment to that effect in the struct svc_pool, so that
>> we don't inadvertently break it later.
>>
> 
> Actually, sashiko points out that struct randomization could break here
> too. Given that service creation isn't a hot codepath, I'd keep these
> as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls and avoid the hassle.
Yeah. It's too clever by half.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: NFSv4.1 COMMIT of all changed areas only on flush? Re: [PATCH 06/10] NFSD: Enable return of an updated stable_how to NFS clients

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 1/2] sunrpc: init gssp_lock before publishing proc entry
Re: [PATCH 2/2] SUNRPC: Check svc pool percpu counter allocation
patches_acked.patch_summary: This patch adds a comment to the svc_pool struct in the SUNRPC code to note that its percpu counter fields must be ordered together, ensuring future modifications do not inadvertently break this requirement.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged reviewer's concern about potential issues with struct randomization breaking the ordering requirements of svc_pool fields, decided to keep the current implementation as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls to avoid added complexity.
review_comments.raw_body:
Actually, sashiko points out that struct randomization could break here
too. Given that service creation isn't a hot codepath, I'd keep these
as discrete percpu_counter_init/destroy() calls and avoid the hassle.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/8] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru
[PATCH v5 0/9] mm: switch THP shrinker to list_lru

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong cgroup when there's a race between adding an object to the LRU and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the effective memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acked the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Reported-by: Sashiko
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---
>  mm/list_lru.c | 26 +++++++++++++++-----------
>  1 file changed, 15 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
> 

Acked-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch, but noted that it's not critical and doesn't need to be backported to stable. Also provided a Reviewed-by tag.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:08PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
>on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
>cgroup that actually has the object.
>
>When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
>parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
>shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
>

This means we just miss to reclaim some obj, but won't crash the kernel.

>This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
>
>Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
>have the caller use that.
>
>Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
>Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>Reported-by: Sashiko
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

So we don't want to cc stable, right?

The fix looks right, so

Reviewed-by: Wei Yang <richard.weiyang@gmail.com>

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function in mm/list_lru.c, which is used to synchronize access to list lru nodes. The patch shares the code between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing duplication.

review_comments.author: Wei Yang
review_comments.summary: Suggested unifying parameter names and annotations in lock_list_lru() for better readability.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
>
>Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>
>Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) <ljs@kernel.org>
>Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
>Reviewed-by: Liam R. Howlett (Oracle) <liam@infradead.org>
>---
> mm/list_lru.c | 21 +++++++++------------
> 1 file changed, 9 insertions(+), 12 deletions(-)
>
>diff --git a/mm/list_lru.c b/mm/list_lru.c
>index 7d0523e44010..fdb3fe2ea64f 100644
>--- a/mm/list_lru.c
>+++ b/mm/list_lru.c
>@@ -15,6 +15,14 @@
> #include "slab.h"
> #include "internal.h"

Hi, Johannes

One very tiny nit below.

> 
>+static inline void lock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq)

Here we use @irq.

>+{
>+	if (irq)
>+		spin_lock_irq(&l->lock);
>+	else
>+		spin_lock(&l->lock);
>+}
>+
> static inline void unlock_list_lru(struct list_lru_one *l, bool irq_off)

Here we use @irq_off.

Do you think it would be nicer to unify the parameter name?

Also the name in callsite and the argument annotation. Would it be cleaner
readers?

-- 
Wei Yang
Help you, Help me

review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 29, 2026 at 09:56:28AM +0000, Wei Yang wrote:
> On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:12PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> >The MEMCG and !MEMCG paths have the same pattern. Share the code.
> >
> >Reviewed-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

Yes, I think it would be nicer.

Note that I inherited this - we had irq on the lock and irq_off on the
unlock before already. I didn't want to mix even more yak shaving prep
patches into this series.

Mind sending a follow-up patch on top of mm-unstable?

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to simplify the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop by flattening it and reducing nesting levels, making the code more readable and maintainable.

review_comments.author: Dev Jain
review_comments.summary: Provided a Reviewed-by tag without any specific technical feedback or suggestions.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a folio is split across multiple nodes, and requested that the list_lru per-memcg heads be instantiated on demand when the first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup.

Reviewer SeongJae Park reported that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error, specifically for the folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() function when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset. They suggested defining the function for this case and provided a temporal fix as evidence.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch is missing a definition for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE is unset, causing an implicit function declaration error in UM mode kunit tests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, 27 May 2026 16:45:16 -0400 Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

---

I found this patch is now in mm-new and it makes UM mode kunit fails like
below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Maybe we can define the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE unset case?  I
confirmed the below attaching temporal fix works for at least kunit.


Thanks,
SJ

[...]
=== >8 ===

---

From: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date: Wed, 27 May 2026 23:58:07 -0700
Subject: [PATCH] mm/huge_mm: define memcg_alloc_deferred() for
 !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE
MIME-Version: 1.0
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8
Content-Transfer-Encoding: 8bit

Without this, UM mode kunit fails like below.

    $ ./tools/testing/kunit/kunit.py run --kunitconfig mm/damon/tests/
    [00:00:02] Configuring KUnit Kernel ...
    [00:00:02] Building KUnit Kernel ...
    Populating config with:
    $ make ARCH=um O=.kunit olddefconfig
    Building with:
    $ make all compile_commands.json scripts_gdb ARCH=um O=.kunit --jobs=8
    ERROR:root:../mm/swap_state.c: In function \u2018__swap_cache_alloc\u2019:
    ../mm/swap_state.c:468:26: error: implicit declaration of function \u2018folio_memcg_alloc_deferred\u2019 [-Wimplicit-function-declaration]
      468 |         if (order > 1 && folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(folio)) {
          |                          ^~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    make[4]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:289: mm/swap_state.o] Error 1
    make[4]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[3]: *** [../scripts/Makefile.build:548: mm] Error 2
    make[3]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....
    make[2]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:2143: .] Error 2
    make[1]: *** [/home/lkhack/linux/Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2
    make: *** [Makefile:248: __sub-make] Error 2

Fix by implementing the function for CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE unset
case.

Fixes: https://lore.kernel.org/20260527204757.2544958-10-hannes@cmpxchg.org
Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
---
 include/linux/huge_mm.h | 5 +++++
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)

diff --git a/include/linux/huge_mm.h b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
index f6c2531a27a35..055de7b8ed487 100644
--- a/include/linux/huge_mm.h
+++ b/include/linux/huge_mm.h
@@ -663,6 +663,11 @@ static inline int folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,
 	return -EINVAL;
 }
 
+static inline int folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(struct folio *folio)
+{
+	return 0;
+}
+
 static inline void deferred_split_folio(struct folio *folio, bool partially_mapped) {}
 #define split_huge_pmd(__vma, __pmd, __address)	\
 	do { } while (0)
-- 
2.47.3

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the necessity of incrementing count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK) in a specific scenario, suggesting either removing it or modifying the control flow to avoid this increment
review_comments.raw_body:
Ah sorry, should have caught this in the previous version, do we need

count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_ANON_FAULT_FALLBACK);

here?

or maybe we just goto next instead of goto fallback and trty next
viable order?

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed Usama Arif's concern that an unexpected failure of a sub-costly-order slab allocation could be missed by the event counter, explaining that such failures are rare and would already be logged in dmesg.

Author addressed a concern about the naming convention for the list_lru allocation, agreeing that 'fallback' is more correct and choosing to leave the code as is.
review_comments.raw_body:
This isn't an allocation we expect to fail with any sort of routine
that we'd need to capture it in the event counter. It would warn in
dmesg if it did. But in practice it can't happen at all, since it's a
sub-costly-order slab allocation and the allocator would loop and OOM
kill stuff until it succeeds.

---

Again I don't think it matters, but fallback seems a bit more correct
because the size of the list_lru allocation doesn't change with lower
orders (until we hit 0).

So I think we can just leave it as is.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a mistake in their testing and promised to add UM (User Mode) builds to their final compile test before resubmitting the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Whoops, thanks for the fix, SJ. I'll incorporate UM builds into my
final compile test before sending.

Acked-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested moving error handling under a shared label for consistency and potential future simplification
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks!

Nit: I think it would be better if we move the error handling under a
label to be shared with the charge failure above, but it's fine this
way, can be simplified later.

Reviewed-by: Kairui Song <kasong@tencent.com>

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

review_comments.author: SeongJae Park
review_comments.summary:
SeongJae Park noted that the patch does not handle the case where a THP is being split and its child folios are still on the list_lru, which can lead to a use-after-free error when the parent folio is freed.

Reviewer SeongJae Park noted that the patch causes a build failure in UM mode due to an implicit function declaration error for folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is unset, and suggested defining the function for this case.

SeongJae Park noted that without the definition of folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() for !CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPPAGE, UM mode kunit fails due to an implicit function declaration error in __swap_cache_alloc(), and provided a patch to define this function.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 4/4] mm: swap: filter swap allocation by memcg tier mask
Re: [PATCH v7 0/4] mm: swap: introduce swap tier infrastructure

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 4/4] mm: swap: filter swap allocation by memcg tier mask
patches_acked.patch_summary: Patch v7 of mm: swap: filter swap allocation by memcg tier mask received a positive review from Nhat Pham, who suggested adding per-cpu caching for each device and proposed a data structure to store swap information.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch as a first step, but raised concerns about the performance implications of adding per-cpu caching for each device. Proposed using a data structure to store swap information and suggested that the extra overhead is minimal.

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH v7 04/15] mm/mglru: restructure the reclaim loop
